tickets.body:
From a project-level adversarial review (Cursor/Grok) focused on reliability, resiliency, and capabilities of the multi-model adversarial-review plugin. Implement the grounded findings below without changing the schema contract except where JSON verdict parity requires overwriting `verdict` (same as multi-provider already does).

FINDINGS TO FIX:
1. HIGH/error-handling — src/llm.js callCliLLM: Claude primary path uses `primaryArgs=[]` while agy gets `["-p","-"]`. Bare claude can hang until the 10m execCli timeout. Fix: treat claude like agy (`["-p","-"]`). Add contract test symmetric to the agy test.
2. HIGH/security — Claude/agy review CLIs lack Codex read-only isolation. Fix: invoke review with `--permission-mode plan` (read-only) plus `-p -`. Add `--allow-unsandboxed-cli` to opt out explicitly. Do NOT change fixer path (`--dangerously-skip-permissions`).
3. HIGH/correctness — src/review.js buildVerifyPrompt defaults to `refuted=true`, lowering gate recall. Fix: invert prior — default `refuted=false`; require concrete contradictory evidence to drop a finding. Update any docs that claim verify-only raises precision without the recall tradeoff.
4. MEDIUM/compatibility — bin/cli.js single-provider `--json` emits model `verdict` while exit code uses `deriveVerdict`. Multi-provider already overwrites. Fix: set `result.verdict = derived.verdict` before JSON emit (keep model disagreement on stderr).
5. MEDIUM/error-handling — execCli hardcodes `timeout: 10*60*1000` and ignores `config.timeoutMs`. Fix: thread timeoutMs through callCliLLM/callCodexCli/execCli.
6. MEDIUM/ci-cd — src/loop.js empty working-tree at fixCount===0 exits 0 even with `--fail-on-empty`. Fix: honor args.failOnEmpty (exit 1) like bin/cli.js.
7. MEDIUM/compatibility — package.json sync-skill only copies prompt-template.md+schema.json into skills/; omits prompt-template-artifact.md and does not sync `.agents/skills/`. Fix: sync artifact template + schema/prompt into both skill trees; extend test/skill-assets.test.js drift guard.
8. LOW/observability — API retries ignore Retry-After. Fix: when status is 429/503 and Retry-After is present, wait that duration (capped) before retry.

Acceptance criteria:
- AC1: claude primary review args include `-p` and `-`; node --test test/llm.test.js covers it.
- AC2: claude/agy review args include `--permission-mode plan`; `--allow-unsandboxed-cli` omits plan mode; fixer path unchanged.
- AC3: buildVerifyPrompt text defaults to keeping findings (refuted=false) unless contradictory evidence; unit assert on prompt text.
- AC4: single-provider path with mocked review where model.verdict=approve but gating finding exists → JSON.verdict=needs-attention AND exit 2 (or unit-level assert that cli overwrites before emit).
- AC5: execCli/llmCall CLI path uses config.timeoutMs (mock binary that sleeps > timeout fails fast).
- AC6: loop empty WT + failOnEmpty → exit 1; without flag still exit 0.
- AC7: sync-skill copies prompt-template-artifact.md; skill-assets.test.js asserts root↔skills and root↔.agents copies match for all three assets.
- AC8: 429 with Retry-After:2 waits ~2s before retry (unit test with fake fetch).
- AC9: npm test green.

Out of scope: changing schema.json required fields; changing fixer sandbox on macOS; new providers; weakening multi-provider quorum.
tickets.title: Resolve grok adversarial-review reliability findings (CLI hang, sandbox, verify bias, JSON/exit parity, timeout, fail-on-empty, skill sync, Retry-After)

tickets.body:
Google deprecated/removed the `gemini` local CLI binary; its replacement is `agy`. This ticket replaces every place the tool auto-detects or documents the *local CLI command* `gemini` so it instead detects/invokes `agy`. The decision is REPLACE ENTIRELY: drop `gemini` from CLI auto-detection lists; only `agy` is auto-detected. Users who still have an old `gemini` binary can still force it explicitly via `--provider gemini` or `--loop-fixer gemini` (handled by the existing generic local-CLI passthrough); no special fallback is added.

CRITICAL DISTINCTION — do NOT touch the Gemini HTTP API:
- The Gemini *API* provider (`GEMINI_API_KEY` -> model `gemini-2.5-pro`, provider name `"gemini"`, `GEMINI_API_BASE`/`GEMINI_BASE_URL`, and the `responseSchema`/`extraDrop` schema handling in `src/schema-validate.js`) is NOT deprecated and MUST remain unchanged. Only the *local CLI command* named `gemini` is being renamed to `agy`.
- The string `gemini` as a provider name in `knownApis` (`src/llm.js`) and in the `--provider` help line listing `anthropic | openai | gemini | cursor` (`src/utils.js:49`) is the API path and MUST stay.

Concrete changes (replace the local-CLI command `gemini` -> `agy`):
1. `src/llm.js` — the three `isCmdInstalled("gemini")` -> `cliCmd = "gemini"` blocks (the Claude-Code env branch ~L252, the Cursor env branch ~L281, and the default auto-detect branch ~L310). Replace the detected command and assigned `cliCmd` with `agy`. Update the three "install a local CLI agent (claude, codex, or gemini)" error messages (~L266, ~L317) and the file-top comment (~L196) to say `agy` instead of `gemini`.
2. `src/loop.js` — the fixer auto-detect loop `["codex", "claude", "gemini"]` (~L145) -> `["codex", "claude", "agy"]`; the "No fixer CLI found (tried codex, claude, gemini)" error (~L149); and `FIXER_PROVIDER_MAP` (~L155) — rename the key `gemini` to `agy` while KEEPING its mapped provider-family value `"gemini"` (so the same-provider/bias check still treats `agy` as the Gemini family). Update the `// gemini or custom` comment in `buildFixerCmd` (~L312) to `// agy or custom`.
3. `src/utils.js` — line ~60 `auto-detect codex->claude->gemini` -> `codex->claude->agy`; line ~70 `a local CLI agent (claude, codex, gemini)` -> `(claude, codex, agy)`. LEAVE line ~49 (`--provider <name> ... anthropic | openai | gemini | cursor`) UNCHANGED (API provider).
4. `README.md` — update references to the local CLI agent `gemini` (e.g. L18 "a local CLI agent like `claude` / `codex` / `gemini`", L70 loop-fixer auto-detect order, L199 "A local CLI agent on `PATH`: `claude`, `codex`, or `gemini`") to use `agy`. LEAVE the Gemini API lines unchanged (L150 `--provider` list, L185 Gemini gateway note, L197 `GEMINI_API_KEY -> Gemini API`).
5. `skills/adversarial-review/SKILL.md` — update the local CLI agent references to `gemini` (description L5, L33-34, and the precedence lists L128/L136/L142 "Local `gemini` CLI" / "local `claude`, `codex`, or `gemini` CLI") to `agy`. LEAVE `GEMINI_API_KEY -> Gemini API` lines (L125, L133, L140) unchanged. Also update the SKILL.md frontmatter `description` if it names the local `gemini` CLI.
6. `test/llm.test.js` — update/add coverage so that local-CLI auto-detection asserts on `agy` (not `gemini`). The existing GEMINI_API_KEY -> provider `gemini` / model `gemini-2.5-pro` API test (~L174-180) MUST remain green and unchanged in intent.

Acceptance criteria (concrete verification):
- AC1: `grep -rniE "\\bgemini\\b" src/ skills/adversarial-review/SKILL.md README.md` shows NO remaining reference to `gemini` used as a *local CLI command*. Remaining `gemini` hits must all be the API provider/model/key/base-url/responseSchema (Gemini API), verified by inspecting each hit.
- AC2: A fresh environment with only `agy` on PATH (and no API keys) auto-selects provider `cli` with `cliCmd === "agy"`. Verify via the unit test in `test/llm.test.js` (mock `isCmdInstalled`).
- AC3: The loop fixer auto-detect picks `agy` when only `agy` is installed, and `FIXER_PROVIDER_MAP["agy"] === "gemini"`.
- AC4: `GEMINI_API_KEY` still resolves to API provider `gemini`, model `gemini-2.5-pro` (existing test unchanged and passing).
- AC5: `npm test` passes (full suite green).

Out of scope: CHANGELOG.md historical entries (do not rewrite history); any change to the Gemini HTTP API behavior, schema handling, or `--provider gemini` semantics.

--- P1 SPEC UPDATE (authoritative spec: .adlc/specs/T1.md) ---
Premortem/interrogation corrected two assumptions in the changes above:
(a) `agy` is a Claude-Code-compatible CLI (`-p`/`--print`, `--dangerously-skip-permissions`), NOT a bare-stdin tool like old `gemini`. It must be invoked like `claude` in BOTH paths: `buildFixerCmd` uses `["--dangerously-skip-permissions","-p","-"]` (not generic `["-"]`); `cliFallbackArgs`/`callCliLLM` use `-p` print mode (bare `agy`+stdin hangs). Provider family stays `gemini` (orthogonal to argv shape).
(b) `FIXER_PROVIDER_MAP`: DROP the `gemini` key entirely; add `agy:"gemini"`. Accepted: forced `--loop-fixer gemini` gets no bias warning.
Also: export `buildFixerCmd`+`FIXER_PROVIDER_MAP` for testing; add new `test/loop.test.js`; widen AC1 grep to the whole repo (excl. node_modules/CHANGELOG). See spec for ACs 1-8.
tickets.title: Replace deprecated `gemini` local CLI agent with `agy` in auto-detection

tickets.body:
Add a first-class Vercel AI Gateway transport so adversarial-review can (a)
authenticate with a single AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY and (b) fan `--providers` across
openai/anthropic/gemini *model families* through that one key — the highest-leverage
Gateway use for a cross-model ship/no-ship gate.

INVESTIGATION FINDINGS (P0, 2026-07-16):
- Today Gateway is only reachable as a generic OpenAI override:
  `--provider openai --api-base <url> --api-key <key>`. Tests mention the legacy host
  `https://gateway.ai.vercel.com/v1/providers/openai`. Current Vercel docs use
  `https://ai-gateway.vercel.sh/v1` + `AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY` (or VERCEL_OIDC_TOKEN)
  with model ids in `provider/model` form (e.g. `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6`,
  `openai/gpt-5.5`, `google/gemini-2.5-pro`). Gateway supports OpenAI Chat Completions
  `response_format.json_schema` — compatible with our existing openai llmCall path
  (including the non-strict gateway fallback already in src/llm.js).
- No auto-detect of AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY. No mapping from family tokens to
  `provider/model` ids. Multi-provider today requires N vendor keys or N local CLIs.
- Package is zero-dependency — do NOT add `ai` / `@ai-sdk/*`; keep raw fetch.

RESOLVED DESIGN DECISIONS:
- D1 Provider id = `vercel` with alias `gateway`. configureLLM({provider:"vercel"|"gateway"})
  uses OpenAI-compatible Chat Completions against apiBase default
  `https://ai-gateway.vercel.sh/v1` (overridable via --api-base / AI_GATEWAY_API_BASE /
  AI_GATEWAY_BASE_URL). Auth: args.apiKey || AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY || VERCEL_OIDC_TOKEN.
  Implementation may reuse the openai fetch branch keyed by an internal
  `apiStyle: "openai"` (or provider==="vercel" alongside openai) — do not duplicate
  the HTTP client.
- D2 Default model for `--provider vercel` = `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6` (strong
  tier; override with --model). Document that models MUST be `provider/model` ids
  when talking to the Gateway.
- D3 One-key multi-family routing: when resolving a family token for `--providers`
  (openai/anthropic/gemini), if the family-specific env key is ABSENT but
  AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY (or VERCEL_OIDC_TOKEN) is PRESENT, resolve that family to a
  vercel/gateway config with the family's default Gateway model id:
    openai    -> openai/gpt-5
    anthropic -> anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6
    gemini    -> google/gemini-2.5-pro
  Prefer native vendor key when BOTH are present (BYOK / direct path wins). Local
  CLI candidates still win over Gateway when the user named a CLI-only token
  (codex/claude/agy). Explicit `--provider openai` with only AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY
  does NOT silently redirect — only the family resolver used by `--providers`
  (and optionally `--providers auto`) gains the Gateway fallback. Single-provider
  users opt in via `--provider vercel`.
- D4 Auto-detect: if no vendor keys and no local CLIs, but AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY is
  set, configureLLM() selects provider=vercel (before throwing "No LLM
  configuration"). Inside Claude Code / Cursor env branches, Gateway counts as an
  independent critic when it can reach a non-builder family (e.g. in Claude Code,
  Gateway→openai or Gateway→gemini is preferred over anthropic).
- D5 Do NOT invent a fourth diversity family named "vercel" — Gateway is a
  TRANSPORT. Diversity remains keyed on underlying model family.

CONCRETE CHANGES:
1. src/llm.js configureLLM: knownApis += vercel/gateway; key/base/model defaults
   per D1/D2; auto-detect per D4.
2. src/llm.js FAMILY_CANDIDATES / resolveProviderToken: insert Gateway fallback
   candidate per D3 (after native API key candidate, before or after CLI —
   RESOLVED: order = native API key > Gateway (if AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY) > local CLI,
   so CI with only a Gateway key gets real API diversity without requiring CLIs).
3. src/llm.js llmCall: route provider vercel/gateway through the existing OpenAI
   chat completions + json_schema path (same sanitizeSchemaForProvider defaults).
4. src/utils.js HELP_TEXT + env docs: AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY, AI_GATEWAY_API_BASE,
   --provider vercel|gateway.
5. README.md + SKILL.md copies: Gateway quickstart + `--providers auto` one-key
   example.
6. Tests: configureLLM vercel defaults; family resolver Gateway fallback when only
   AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY set yields 3 distinct families; native key preferred over
   Gateway; provider=openai with only Gateway key still errors (no silent redirect);
   utils help/env parsing if any new flags (none required beyond provider name).

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA (concrete verification):
- AC1: configureLLM({provider:"vercel"}) with AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY=test returns
  provider=vercel (or gateway normalized), apiBase=https://ai-gateway.vercel.sh/v1,
  model=anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6, apiKey=test. Verify: test/llm.test.js.
- AC2: Alias `--provider gateway` resolves identically to vercel. Verify: unit test.
- AC3: With ONLY AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY set (no OPENAI_/ANTHROPIC_/GEMINI_ keys, no
  CLIs), `--providers auto` (or explicit gpt,claude,gemini) resolves >=2 distinct
  families all using the Gateway transport and distinct provider/model ids.
  Verify: test/multiprovider.test.js with env stubbing.
- AC4: With OPENAI_API_KEY and AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY both set, openai family uses
  native api.openai.com (or OPENAI_API_BASE), not the Gateway. Verify: unit test.
- AC5: configureLLM({provider:"openai"}) with only AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY set throws /
  errors (no silent redirect on explicit openai). Verify: unit test.
- AC6: Auto-detect with only AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY selects vercel. Verify: unit test.
- AC7: Documentation updated (README + SKILL.md) mentions AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY and
  provider/model ids. Verify: grep in test or checklist in PR.
- AC8: npm test exits 0.

OUT OF SCOPE: adding AI SDK packages; Anthropic-native Messages endpoint via
Gateway (OpenAI-compat is enough); BYOK dashboard config; Cursor provider work (T9);
changing quorum/merge semantics.

Depends on T9 only for merge ordering (both touch src/llm.js) — edge lives on T9.
tickets.title: Add first-class Vercel AI Gateway provider and one-key multi-family routing

tickets.body:
Ported from the peer project Robbyfuu/codex-plugin-cc ("peer"), commit 02d4b4c "fix(codex): fence untrusted content in Codex prompts against injection", file plugins/codex/scripts/lib/prompts.mjs. Full comparative analysis with file:line evidence is in docs/peer-port-plan.md section A3. NOTE: the two projects share no git history (no merge-base) — this is a hand port, not a cherry-pick.

PROBLEM: We interpolate untrusted content directly into the instruction body of our review prompts. The reviewed git diff (REVIEW_INPUT) and the user focus text (USER_FOCUS) are attacker-influenceable — anyone who can land text in a reviewed diff can land text in the model's instruction position and steer the verdict. There is currently no fencing anywhere: grep for 'UNTRUSTED' or 'fenceUntrusted' across src/ and prompt-template*.md returns nothing.

Our exposure is LARGER than peer's: we also have `--input` artifact mode (src/review.js buildArtifactPrompt, added for .adlc ticket T6) which reviews user-supplied spec/ticket files. Peer has no equivalent mode. Both builders must be fenced.

IMPLEMENTATION:

1. Add `fenceUntrusted(label, value)` to src/review.js (exported, pure, does not mutate inputs).

2. Port peer's stripper semantics exactly — it is stronger than a naive 'strip the closing tag' approach in three specific ways, all of which are required:
   a. Strip BOTH forged opening and forged closing markers. Stripping only the closing marker leaves an injected opening marker able to spoof a second fence.
   b. Match label-agnostically. Any sentinel token is a breakout risk regardless of the label it carries.
   c. Also strip bare sentinel prefixes left without a closing '>>>', so a truncated/half-formed marker cannot linger.
   Peer's implementation (prompts.mjs) for reference:
     value.replace(/<<<UNTRUSTED:[^>]*>>>/g, "").replace(/<<<END:[^>]*>>>/g, "")
          .split("<<<UNTRUSTED:").join("").split("<<<END:").join("")

3. IMPROVE ON PEER: peer's sentinels are static compile-time constants, so anyone who has read their (public, Apache-2.0) source knows the exact token to forge. Generate a per-run random nonce and include it in the emitted fence: `<<<UNTRUSTED:{label}:{nonce}>>>` / `<<<END:{label}:{nonce}>>>`. Use crypto.randomUUID() or crypto.randomBytes(9).toString('base64url'). Keep the stripping label-agnostic AND nonce-agnostic (strip any sentinel-shaped token, not just the current nonce).

4. Fence ONLY the untrusted variables — REVIEW_INPUT and USER_FOCUS — in BOTH buildPrompt and buildArtifactPrompt. Leave trusted scaffolding (schema reference, guidance, severity definitions, target labels) unfenced. Peer made this same split deliberately; do not over-fence.

5. Add a one-line preamble to prompt-template.md and prompt-template-artifact.md stating that fenced content is data to analyze, never instructions.

6. package.json `sync-skill` copies prompt-template.md, prompt-template-artifact.md and schema.json into skills/ and .agents/skills/. Template edits MUST be followed by a sync or test/skill-assets.test.js (the drift guard) will fail.

OUT OF SCOPE: changing schema.json required fields (ours requires `coverage`, peer's does not — do not align them); changing the fixer prompt path in src/loop.js; any provider/transport change.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- AC1: fenceUntrusted(label, value) wraps value in an opening and closing sentinel that both carry the label and a per-run nonce. VERIFY: unit test in test/review.test.js asserts the returned string starts with a marker containing the label, ends with a closing marker containing the label, and that two calls in separate runs (re-imported module or injected nonce seam) emit different nonces.
- AC2: A forged CLOSING sentinel inside the untrusted value cannot terminate the fence early. VERIFY: unit test passes a value containing '<<<END:REVIEW_INPUT>>>' and asserts that substring is absent from the output between the real fences.
- AC3: A forged OPENING sentinel inside the untrusted value is also stripped. VERIFY: unit test passes a value containing '<<<UNTRUSTED:REVIEW_INPUT>>>' and asserts it is absent from the output.
- AC4: Stripping is label-agnostic. VERIFY: unit test passes a value containing '<<<END:SOMETHING_ELSE>>>' and asserts it is stripped.
- AC5: A bare sentinel prefix with no closing '>>>' is stripped. VERIFY: unit test passes a value containing the bare string '<<<UNTRUSTED:' and asserts it is absent from the output.
- AC6: buildPrompt fences REVIEW_INPUT and USER_FOCUS and does not fence the schema/guidance scaffolding. VERIFY: unit test builds a prompt with a known diff and known focus text and asserts both appear inside fences while a known scaffolding string appears outside any fence.
- AC7: buildArtifactPrompt fences the same variables. VERIFY: unit test symmetric to AC6 against the artifact builder.
- AC8: Both templates carry the data-not-instructions preamble. VERIFY: unit test asserts the preamble string is present in each template file as loaded by the builders.
- AC9: Skill asset copies stay in sync after the template edits. VERIFY: node --test test/skill-assets.test.js exits 0.
- AC10: Full suite green. VERIFY: npm test exits 0.

AMENDMENTS (P2 coldstart gate):
- NONCE SEAM + SCOPE (resolves an inconsistency between the declared signature and AC1): the signature is fenceUntrusted(label, value, { nonce } = {}). A fresh nonce is generated per CALL when not supplied; the optional nonce parameter exists so tests can pin it deterministically. AC1 is verified by calling twice without a nonce and asserting the emitted sentinels differ, NOT by re-importing the module.
- The preamble added to the templates must NOT quote a literal nonce value (it changes every run); it refers to the fence markers generically.

AMENDMENTS (P2 coldstart gate):
- NONCE SEAM + SCOPE (resolves an inconsistency between the declared signature and AC1): the signature is fenceUntrusted(label, value, { nonce } = {}). A fresh nonce is generated per CALL when not supplied; the optional nonce parameter exists so tests can pin it deterministically. AC1 is therefore verified by calling twice without a nonce and asserting the emitted sentinels differ — NOT by re-importing the module.
- The preamble added to the templates must NOT quote a literal nonce value (it changes every run); it refers to the fence markers generically.
tickets.title: Fence untrusted content in review prompts against injection (PORT-1)

tickets.body:
Ported from peer project Robbyfuu/codex-plugin-cc, commits c1cc2b4 "never select the Windows spawn shell from process.env.SHELL" and 109ff6d "guard terminateProcessTree against stale, reused, and non-positive PIDs", both consolidated in plugins/codex/scripts/lib/process.mjs. Analysis in docs/peer-port-plan.md sections A1 and A4. No shared git history — hand port.

PROBLEM 1 (security, live defect): src/llm.js:203 passes `shell: process.platform === "win32"` to execFileSync. On Windows every argument we hand a local CLI is re-parsed by cmd.exe, losing argv metacharacter safety. Peer had the same construct and removed it; their rule is shell:false on every platform, and if a Windows binary genuinely cannot resolve without a shell the ONLY permitted fallback is process.env.ComSpec (cmd.exe), never process.env.SHELL (attacker-influenceable, inherited into child processes).

COMPLICATION — DO NOT SIMPLY FLIP IT TO FALSE. Our `shell: true` on Windows exists for a reason: it is how npm-installed CLIs (.cmd shims) resolve. Peer hit the identical history (their cf6f851 added shell:true on Windows to resolve .cmd shims, and c1cc2b4 later removed it). Flipping the flag without replacing the resolution mechanism breaks every Windows user with an npm-installed claude/codex/agy/agent.

CORRECT PORT: resolve the binary to an absolute path WITH extension before spawning, then spawn that resolved path with shell:false unconditionally. src/llm.js:170-192 `isCmdInstalled(cmd)` already walks PATH and PATHEXT and computes exactly the candidate path we need — it just discards it and returns a boolean. Refactor it to return the resolved path (or null), keep a thin boolean wrapper if existing callers need it, and spawn the resolved path.

PROBLEM 2 (prerequisite for T13): we have no process-tree termination anywhere in the codebase. T13's watchdog is worthless without it — killing only the direct child leaves the CLI's own grandchildren running, which is precisely the hang being fixed. Two subtleties from peer worth porting verbatim in spirit:
  - Kill the process GROUP, not the pid: POSIX `kill(-pid, 'SIGTERM')` with a bare-pid fallback; Windows `taskkill /PID <pid> /T /F` with a `kill(pid)` fallback on ENOENT.
  - Never signal a stale or recycled pid. Require pid > 0 before signalling, because kill(0, ...) and kill(-0, ...) are group-relative and would target OUR OWN process group. Probe liveness with signal 0: ESRCH means gone; any other error (e.g. EPERM) means the process exists and we simply cannot signal it, so treat it as alive.

IMPLEMENTATION: create src/spawn-safe.js exporting:
  - resolveCommand(cmd) -> absolute path string or null (extracted from isCmdInstalled)
  - isPidAlive(pid, killImpl?) -> boolean
  - terminateProcessTree(pid, { platform?, runCommandImpl?, killImpl? }) -> { attempted, delivered, method }
Inject the platform/kill/run seams as optional parameters exactly as peer does — that is what makes this unit-testable without spawning real process trees or waiting on real signals, and it directly serves .adlc ticket T8 (de-flake the spawn-heavy tests).

Then update src/llm.js:195-205 execCli to spawn the resolved path with shell:false.

OUT OF SCOPE: the watchdog itself and any streaming (T13); changing timeouts; changing which CLIs we detect; changing src/loop.js fixer spawning (a later ticket may reuse spawn-safe there, but not here).

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- AC1: No spawn site in src/ passes a truthy `shell` derived from platform or from any environment variable. VERIFY: grep -n 'shell:' src/*.js shows only `shell: false` or no shell key; assert in a unit test that execCli's spawn options have shell === false.
- AC2: resolveCommand returns an absolute path for a command present on PATH and null for one that is not. VERIFY: unit test in test/spawn-safe.test.js against a real known binary (node) and a guaranteed-absent name.
- AC3: resolveCommand honors PATHEXT on win32 so .cmd shims resolve. VERIFY: unit test injects a fake platform + PATH + PATHEXT and a temp dir containing 'foo.cmd', asserting the resolved path ends in 'foo.cmd'.
- AC4: isPidAlive returns false for pid 0, -0, negative, NaN, null and undefined WITHOUT calling the kill implementation. VERIFY: unit test passes a killImpl spy that throws if invoked, and asserts false for each input and zero spy calls.
- AC5: isPidAlive treats ESRCH as dead and EPERM as alive. VERIFY: unit test injects a killImpl throwing each code and asserts false/true respectively.
- AC6: terminateProcessTree does not signal anything when the pid is not alive. VERIFY: unit test with a killImpl that reports ESRCH on the liveness probe asserts the result is { attempted: false, delivered: false, method: null } and that no termination call followed.
- AC7: On POSIX, terminateProcessTree signals the process group (negative pid) first. VERIFY: unit test with platform 'linux' and a killImpl spy asserts the first termination call received -pid.
- AC8: On win32, terminateProcessTree shells out to taskkill with /T and /F. VERIFY: unit test with platform 'win32' and a runCommandImpl spy asserts the argv contains '/T' and '/F' and the pid.
- AC9: Existing local-CLI invocation still works end to end after the resolved-path change. VERIFY: node --test test/llm.test.js exits 0, including the existing mock-CLI contract tests.
- AC10: Full suite green. VERIFY: npm test exits 0.

AMENDMENTS (P2 coldstart gate):
- RESOLVER SEAM (resolves an inconsistency between the declared signature and AC3): the signature is resolveCommand(cmd, { platform, env } = {}), defaulting to process.platform and process.env. AC3 injects platform:'win32' plus a synthetic env carrying PATH and PATHEXT; without this seam AC3 is unverifiable on a POSIX CI machine.
- NOT-FOUND BEHAVIOR (previously unspecified): when resolveCommand returns null, execCli MUST throw a clear error naming the command and stating it was not found on PATH. It MUST NOT fall back to a shell, and MUST NOT pass the bare command name to spawn hoping the OS resolves it. Add AC11: unit test asserts the thrown message names the missing command.
tickets.title: spawn-safe: never select the Windows shell from env; add pid-safe process-tree termination (PORT-2)

tickets.body:
Ported from peer project Robbyfuu/codex-plugin-cc, commit 5545215 "add timeouts, broker self-heal, and live tmux visibility", reference implementation plugins/codex/scripts/lib/watchdog.mjs (createIdleWatchdog, resolveTimeouts, CodexStallError, CodexTimeoutError). Analysis in docs/peer-port-plan.md section A2. No shared git history — hand port.

DEPENDS ON T12: this ticket consumes terminateProcessTree and isPidAlive from src/spawn-safe.js. Do not start until T12 has merged; a watchdog that kills only the direct child leaves grandchildren alive and does not actually fix the hang.

PROBLEM: src/llm.js:195-205 invokes local CLIs with blocking execFileSync guarded only by a single wall-clock `timeout`. A CLI that hangs consumes the entire timeout (default 120s via --timeout, up to 10 minutes on some paths) with zero output and no diagnosis. Related shipped fix: commit 1183211 caught ETIMEDOUT and raised the default, which treated the symptom.

CRITICAL DESIGN CONSTRAINT — A NAIVE IDLE TIMER IS WRONG. The obvious design (reset a timer on every stdout/stderr chunk; kill when it expires) false-trips and kills healthy work. Peer hit this and documented it in watchdog.mjs: a long-running item emits a start event and then NOTHING until it completes, so a naive 'no output for N seconds means stalled' guard kills legitimate long operations. Our exposure is worse than theirs — we read raw stdout from four different CLIs (claude, codex, agy, agent) and a local agent can easily spend minutes on a silent tool call.

REQUIRED DESIGN — TWO INDEPENDENT GUARDS, NOT ONE:
  1. idleMs (default 180000): fires a stall error when there has been no output for the window.
  2. maxMs hard ceiling (default 900000): fires regardless of activity. This is the real backstop and the only thing that can end a genuinely stuck run.
The hard ceiling is mandatory. The idle guard is an optimisation that returns a faster, better-diagnosed error in the common case; it must never be the only guard.

We CANNOT port peer's in-flight item-awareness directly: they get structured item/started and item/completed events from a JSON-RPC app-server stream, and we read unstructured stdout. Consuming codex's JSONL event stream to recover true item-awareness for the codex path is a deliberate FOLLOW-ON, explicitly out of scope here — do not bundle it. Set idleMs generously precisely because we lack that signal.

BOUNDED BUFFER (mandatory): the function returns buffered stdout on success. It MUST cap that buffer and fail loudly on overflow. The current execFileSync path already has maxBuffer: 10 * 1024 * 1024 (src/llm.js:201); an unbounded replacement would be a memory regression that lets a runaway CLI exhaust the process before any timer fires. Keep a 10 MB cap.

TESTABILITY (mandatory): inject setTimeout/clearTimeout seams as peer does (setTimeoutImpl / clearTimeoutImpl parameters). This is what lets the timing behavior be unit-tested with fake timers instead of real wall-clock sleeps, and it directly serves .adlc ticket T8 (de-flake spawn-heavy tests under parallel load). Unref every timer so a pending watchdog never holds the event loop open. Make both signals one-shot and terminal, and stop() idempotent and clearing every timer.

IMPLEMENTATION:
1. src/exec-watchdog.js exports spawnWithWatchdog(cmd, args, { input, timeoutMs, idleTimeoutMs, maxBuffer, streamStdout, setTimeoutImpl, clearTimeoutImpl }) built on child_process.spawn. Pipes input to stdin, accumulates stdout/stderr, resets the idle timer on each chunk, arms the hard ceiling once at start, and on either expiry calls terminateProcessTree from src/spawn-safe.js then rejects with a distinguishable error (code EIDLE for the idle guard, ETIMEDOUT for the ceiling).
2. When streamStdout is true, route chunks to process.stdout as they arrive while still accumulating for the return value.
3. src/utils.js: add a boolean --stream flag to parseArgs and document it in HELP_TEXT. NOTE: the --profile flag from the superseded booster plan is deliberately NOT included — this repo has no config-file mechanism of any kind (no cosmiconfig, no rc file, no package.json key), so a profile flag would override nothing. If profiles are wanted that is a separate config-system design ticket.
4. src/llm.js: replace the execFileSync local-CLI path with spawnWithWatchdog. Thread the existing config.timeoutMs (--timeout) through as the hard ceiling. Plumb args.stream to streamStdout. Catch the idle error and surface a user-facing message naming the idle window and suggesting --timeout / --stream, distinct from the existing exceeded-timeout message.
5. Do NOT change src/loop.js fixer spawning in this ticket.

OUT OF SCOPE: codex JSONL event-stream consumption; API-provider (HTTP) timeouts; --profile; loop fixer spawning; any change to which CLIs are detected.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- AC1: spawnWithWatchdog returns buffered stdout for a process that exits 0. VERIFY: unit test in test/exec-watchdog.test.js spawns `node -e "process.stdout.write('ok')"` and asserts the resolved value is 'ok'.
- AC2: input is piped to the child's stdin. VERIFY: unit test spawns a node one-liner that echoes stdin and asserts the round trip.
- AC3: The idle guard fires when no output arrives for idleTimeoutMs. VERIFY: unit test injects setTimeoutImpl/clearTimeoutImpl fakes, advances past the idle window with no chunks, and asserts rejection with code EIDLE — with NO real sleep in the test.
- AC4: Output resets the idle guard. VERIFY: unit test with injected timers emits chunks at intervals shorter than idleTimeoutMs across a span longer than it, and asserts no EIDLE rejection.
- AC5: The hard ceiling fires even while output is still arriving. VERIFY: unit test with injected timers emits continuous chunks past maxMs and asserts rejection with code ETIMEDOUT. This is the test that proves the ceiling is independent of the idle guard.
- AC6: Buffer overflow fails loudly rather than growing unbounded. VERIFY: unit test sets a small maxBuffer, has the child emit more than that, and asserts a rejection naming the cap (not a truncated success).
- AC7: On either timer expiry, terminateProcessTree is invoked for the child pid. VERIFY: unit test injects a terminateProcessTree spy and asserts it was called with the spawned pid.
- AC8: streamStdout true writes chunks to process.stdout AND still returns them. VERIFY: unit test captures process.stdout.write and asserts both the side effect and the returned buffer.
- AC9: Every timer is unref'd so a pending watchdog cannot hold the event loop open. VERIFY: unit test asserts unref was called on the timer objects returned by the injected setTimeoutImpl.
- AC10: --stream parses as a boolean and appears in HELP_TEXT. VERIFY: unit test in test/utils.test.js asserts parseArgs(['--stream']).stream === true and that HELP_TEXT contains '--stream'.
- AC11: src/llm.js local-CLI path uses spawnWithWatchdog and threads config.timeoutMs as the ceiling. VERIFY: test/llm.test.js mocks spawnWithWatchdog and asserts it receives the configured timeout; a mock CLI that sleeps past the timeout fails fast rather than hanging.
- AC12: The idle error message is distinct from the wall-clock timeout message and names the idle window. VERIFY: unit test asserts the surfaced message text for an EIDLE rejection differs from the ETIMEDOUT message and contains the idle duration.
- AC13: Full suite green, and the loop e2e tests are not made flakier. VERIFY: npm test exits 0 on three consecutive runs.

AMENDMENTS (P2 coldstart gate):
- CEILING CONFLICT RESOLVED (the body previously specified BOTH a 900000ms default ceiling AND threading config.timeoutMs as the ceiling; our --timeout default is 120s, so those contradict): the hard ceiling IS config.timeoutMs whenever a timeout is configured. DEFAULT_MAX_MS (900000) applies ONLY as the fallback when no timeout is configured. Additionally the effective idle window MUST be clamped strictly below the ceiling — otherwise a user passing --timeout 60 gets a 180s idle guard that can never fire, silently collapsing the design back to a single guard. Add AC14: with a 60s configured timeout, assert the effective idle window is < 60s and the ceiling is 60s.
- ERROR MUST CARRY STDERR (cross-ticket contract with T14): on ANY rejection (EIDLE, ETIMEDOUT, non-zero exit, buffer overflow) the thrown error MUST expose the captured stderr and any partial stdout as properties. T14 parses that stderr for resume ids; if the error discards it, T14 cannot be implemented at all. Add AC15: assert a rejected run exposes the stderr captured before the failure.
- spawnWithWatchdog returns a Promise: AC1/AC2 resolve it, the timer ACs assert rejection.
tickets.title: Anti-hang watchdog for local CLI invocation: idle guard + hard ceiling + --stream (PORT-3)

tickets.body:
Two small independent items ported from peer project Robbyfuu/codex-plugin-cc. Analysis in docs/peer-port-plan.md sections B1 and A5. No shared git history — hand port.

ITEM 1 — RESUME IDS (peer commit acf6437 "surface --resume-id on liveness-reconciled failed jobs", plugins/codex/scripts/lib/render.mjs).

When a local CLI run dies (timeout, non-zero exit, idle stall), the underlying agent session frequently still exists and is resumable, but we discard that entirely — the user sees a failure with no path back to the work already done.

IMPORTANT MECHANISM DIFFERENCE — DO NOT COPY PEER'S APPROACH LITERALLY. Peer reads the resume id from a STRUCTURED tracked-job record carrying a threadId, and their change is render-only, gated on job status (failed/interrupted with a thread; never completed or cancelled). We have no job store and no persisted session state, so text-scraping the CLI's stderr/stdout is the only mechanism available to us. It is strictly weaker than peer's and the implementation must treat it as such:
  - BEST EFFORT ONLY. A missing or unparseable resume line must never fail the run, never change the exit code, and never emit a warning that could be mistaken for an error.
  - Gate on failure states only, mirroring peer's status gate. Never emit a resume hint for a successful review.
  - Anchor the pattern PER CLI (codex vs agy vs claude vs agent) rather than one loose global regex, so an unrelated string in a diff cannot be mistaken for a session id.
  - Cap the amount of stderr scanned (e.g. last 8 KB) so a huge error dump cannot cause a pathological scan.
  - Validate the extracted id shape (bounded length, no whitespace/control characters) before echoing it back to the terminal.

When found, log a clear 'Resume here: <cmd> resume <id>' line and include the resume hint in the loop_summary NDJSON terminal event (src/loop.js) so it survives into CI evidence alongside the existing providers/iterations/verdict/exitReason/survivingCount/acceptedCount fields.

NOTE: the superseded booster plan named test/cli.test.mjs for this work. THAT FILE DOES NOT EXIST. The CLI tests are test/cli-multiprovider.test.js; the loop summary tests are test/loop-summary.test.mjs.

ITEM 2 — LEDGER FILE MODES (peer commit c9d0c62 "harden broker socket and state-dir permissions to 0700").

src/findings-ledger.js:39-41 creates the ledger directory and appends the ledger with no explicit permission modes:
    if (dir && dir !== ".") fs.mkdirSync(dir, { recursive: true });
    fs.appendFileSync(ledgerPath, buffer);
Gating findings routinely quote source code, so the ledger can contain excerpts of the reviewed repository. Create the directory with mode 0o700 and the file with mode 0o600.

CONTEXT — WHAT IS ALREADY DONE, DO NOT REDO: the booster plan claimed src/llm.js needed the same hardening. It does not. src/llm.js:307 already writes the codex temp schema file with { mode: 0o600, flag: 'wx' } inside a directory created by fs.mkdtempSync, which Node already creates as 0o700. That path is correct as-is; the findings ledger is the actual gap.

OUT OF SCOPE: changing the ledger's JSONL record shape or its default path (.adlc/findings.jsonl); adding a job/session store; changing exit codes for any existing condition.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- AC1: A codex-style resume line in captured stderr is extracted and surfaced. VERIFY: unit test feeds a stderr fixture containing a codex resume line and asserts the emitted message contains 'Resume here:' and the session id.
- AC2: An agy-style resume line is extracted by its own anchored pattern. VERIFY: unit test symmetric to AC1 with an agy fixture.
- AC3: Absent or unparseable resume output changes nothing. VERIFY: unit test feeds stderr with no resume line and asserts no resume message is emitted, the returned exit code is unchanged, and no throw occurs.
- AC4: No resume hint is emitted on a successful run. VERIFY: unit test drives a success path whose output happens to contain a resume-shaped string and asserts nothing is surfaced.
- AC5: A malformed or oversized candidate id is rejected rather than echoed. VERIFY: unit test feeds a resume-shaped line whose id contains whitespace/control characters or exceeds the length bound, and asserts no resume message is emitted.
- AC6: Only a bounded tail of stderr is scanned. VERIFY: unit test feeds stderr larger than the cap with a valid resume line placed only in the discarded head, and asserts nothing is surfaced.
- AC7: The loop_summary NDJSON terminal event carries the resume hint when one was found. VERIFY: test/loop-summary.test.mjs asserts the loop_summary event includes the resume field on a failing run and omits or nulls it otherwise.
- AC8: The findings-ledger directory is created 0o700 and the file 0o600. VERIFY: test in test/findings-ledger.test.js writes a ledger under a temp dir and asserts fs.statSync(...).mode & 0o777 equals 0o700 for the directory and 0o600 for the file. Skip the assertion on win32, where POSIX mode bits are not meaningful.
- AC9: Appending to an existing ledger does not loosen its mode. VERIFY: test appends twice and re-asserts 0o600 after the second append.
- AC10: Full suite green. VERIFY: npm test exits 0.
tickets.title: Surface best-effort resume IDs on CLI failure; harden findings-ledger file modes (PORT-4)

tickets.body:
Found by adversarial-review reviewing its own T11 branch (feat/t11-prompt-fencing), MEDIUM/security, confidence 0.55. Deliberately excluded from T11's scope — T11's body states the loop fixer path is out of scope — because a drive-by change to the WRITE path during a review-path ticket is how scope creep ships bugs. Filed as its own ticket instead. Depends on T11 landing, since it consumes fenceUntrusted from src/review.js.

PROBLEM: T11 fenced every untrusted value on the READ path (buildPrompt, buildArtifactPrompt, buildVerifyPrompt). The WRITE path was left unfenced, and it is the higher-consequence sink of the two.

buildFixPrompt (src/loop.js:306-332) interpolates model-derived finding fields directly into the instruction body of the fixer prompt:
  - f.title      -> '## Finding N: <title>'
  - f.body       -> 'Issue: <body>'
  - f.recommendation -> 'Fix: <recommendation>'
  - f.file / line_start / line_end -> 'Location: <loc>'
  - the derived `files` list -> '## Files to Edit'

That prompt is then piped to a fixer spawned by buildFixerCmd (src/loop.js:336-362) as:
  - claude / agy: ['--dangerously-skip-permissions', '-p', '-']
  - codex:        ['exec', '--ephemeral', '--ignore-rules', '-']
i.e. a write-capable agent with permission prompts disabled. On macOS there is no write sandbox at all (--loop-unsafe is required precisely because of this); on Linux the unshare wrapper is best-effort.

ATTACK CHAIN (each link already exists in the codebase):
1. Attacker lands text in a reviewed diff.
2. The review model, summarizing that diff, emits a finding whose free-text field (recommendation is the softest target — it is prose the model is expected to paraphrase from the diff) carries attacker-chosen instructions.
3. buildFixPrompt copies that text verbatim into the fixer's instruction body.
4. The fixer executes it with write access and no permission gate.
Note step 2 does not require the review model to be 'fooled' in any deep sense — recommendation is expected to restate content derived from the diff.

CRITICAL DESIGN CONSTRAINT — DO NOT COPY THE READ-PATH FENCE SEMANTICS. On the read path the instruction is 'this is data, never instructions'. That is exactly WRONG here: the fixer's entire job is to act on the recommendation. A naive port of the review preamble would either be ignored (because it contradicts the fixer's task) or would break fixing altogether. The fence semantics for this path must instead be scoped authority:
  - finding text describes WHAT to fix and is to be acted on;
  - it may NOT redirect the fixer's operating rules, expand the editable file set, request command execution, alter tool permissions, or change the commit/output contract;
  - the authoritative file list and the 'only edit the files listed above' constraint come from OUR scaffolding, outside the fence, and always win over anything inside it.
Write the preamble to say that explicitly rather than reusing the review wording.

IMPLEMENTATION:
1. Import fenceUntrusted from src/review.js (already exported by T11).
2. In buildFixPrompt, fence each untrusted span. Prefer fencing the per-finding block as one unit with a distinct label (e.g. FINDING_N) over fencing each field separately — fewer markers, and the fixer needs the fields grouped to act on them coherently.
3. Keep the trusted scaffolding OUTSIDE the fences: the role line, the '## Files to Edit' list, and the 'Only edit the files listed above.' constraint. The file list is derived from finding.file values, so sanitize those paths rather than fencing them: reject or drop entries that are absolute, contain '..', or escape the repo root. A fenced path is still a path the fixer will act on — fencing does not sanitize it.
4. Add the scoped-authority preamble described above.
5. Do NOT change buildFixerCmd, the sandbox/constraint logic, the secret-scan behavior at src/loop.js:572-587, or any timeout.

OUT OF SCOPE: changing the fixer sandbox model on macOS; adding new fixer CLIs; changing the review path (done in T11); changing the loop's iteration or commit logic.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- AC1: Each finding's untrusted free-text is fenced in the generated fix prompt. VERIFY: unit test in test/loop.test.js builds a fix prompt from a finding whose title/body/recommendation carry marker strings and asserts each appears inside an <<<UNTRUSTED:...>>> / <<<END:...>>> pair.
- AC2: A forged sentinel inside finding text cannot terminate the fence early. VERIFY: unit test sets recommendation to a string containing '<<<END:FINDING_1>>>' and asserts the emitted body contains no forgeable sentinel prefix while the surrounding recommendation text survives.
- AC3: Fencing is non-destructive across newlines. VERIFY: unit test sets body to a multi-line string containing a forged marker on one line and '>>>' several lines later, and asserts every original line still appears (the T11 span-deletion regression must not be reintroduced on this path).
- AC4: The trusted constraint text stays outside the fences. VERIFY: unit test asserts 'Only edit the files listed above.' and the '## Files to Edit' header are not inside any fence.
- AC5: The scoped-authority preamble is present and does NOT say finding text is never to be acted on. VERIFY: unit test asserts the preamble matches the scoped wording and does not contain the review path's 'data to analyze, never instructions' phrasing, which would contradict the fixer's task.
- AC6: Path traversal in a finding's file field never reaches the editable file list. VERIFY: unit test passes findings citing '../../etc/passwd', '/etc/passwd' and 'ok/file.js' and asserts only 'ok/file.js' appears in the '## Files to Edit' section.
- AC7: The fixer is still invoked with an unchanged command line. VERIFY: existing buildFixerCmd tests pass unmodified (guards against scope creep into the spawn path).
- AC8: Full suite green. VERIFY: npm test exits 0.
- AC9: The change is prosecuted. VERIFY: adlc hollow-test over the changed loop code kills its mutants or records an explicit equivalent-mutant rationale, and an adversarial-review pass over the branch returns no gating finding on this path.
tickets.title: Fence the write-capable loop fixer prompt (highest-consequence injection sink)

tickets.body:
Found by adversarial-review round 5 on the T11 branch, HIGH/injection, confidence 0.94 (codex). Deliberately not fixed in T11 — see WHY DEFERRED below.

PROBLEM: T11 added a TRUST_POLICY (src/review.js) declaring that all repository-derived content is untrusted data, including content the reviewer reads itself via git or file tools. For API providers that policy is delivered as a real system instruction and carries system-level priority. For LOCAL CLI providers it does not: callCliLLM (src/llm.js:367-372) concatenates it into the user prompt:

    let fullPrompt = "";
    if (systemInstruction) {
      fullPrompt += `System Instructions:\n${systemInstruction}\n\n`;
    }
    fullPrompt += `Prompt:\n${prompt}`;

So the text labelled 'System Instructions' is ordinary user-prompt content with no elevated priority. Plan/read-only modes (--permission-mode plan, --mode plan) constrain WRITES; they do not raise instruction priority and do not disable project-rule discovery.

WHY THIS MATTERS MOST HERE: local CLI agents are exactly the providers that perform unfenced tool reads. On a summary-only review (diff exceeds --max-files/--max-bytes) the prompt instructs the agent to inspect the diff itself with git. That content arrives with no fence markers, and the rule telling the agent to distrust it has no more authority than the hostile content itself. An attacker can force this path simply by making the diff large enough.

WHY DEFERRED FROM T11: (a) `claude` supports --append-system-prompt, but `agy` exposes no equivalent and `codex exec` takes instructions only via stdin — a claude-only patch would cover one of three providers while implying the hole is closed; (b) the change lands in the CLI arg-construction and spawn path that T12 (spawn-safe) and T13 (watchdog) are rewriting, so doing it in T11 would guarantee a conflict.

DEPENDS ON: T12 and T13 landing first, since both rewrite the spawn path this touches.

IMPLEMENTATION DIRECTIONS (evaluate, do not assume):
1. Use each CLI's real system/developer-prompt mechanism where one exists (claude: --append-system-prompt). Verify per CLI against its current --help rather than trusting this ticket; the flags change between releases.
2. Where no privileged channel exists (agy, codex today), consider whether the policy can be strengthened structurally instead: run the CLI from an isolated working directory so repository content reaches it only through the controlled prompt, and/or disable project-rule discovery where the CLI supports it (codex already gets --ignore-rules).
3. Whatever ships must degrade honestly: if a provider cannot be given a privileged channel, that limitation should be visible to the operator, not silently absent.

OUT OF SCOPE: changing the fence format or TRUST_POLICY wording (T11); the fixer path (T15); the grounding check (GitHub #30).

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- AC1: For every local CLI where a privileged system-prompt mechanism exists, the trust policy is delivered through it rather than concatenated into the user prompt. VERIFY: unit test asserts the constructed argv for that CLI carries the system text in the privileged flag and that the user prompt no longer contains the 'System Instructions:' prefix.
- AC2: CLIs without such a mechanism still receive the policy and the operator can tell. VERIFY: unit test asserts the fallback path still includes the policy AND that a warning naming the provider is emitted.
- AC3: Flag support is detected against the installed CLI, not assumed. VERIFY: unit test simulates a CLI rejecting the flag (unknown-flag stderr) and asserts a clear error or a documented fallback, not a silent unprotected review.
- AC4: An end-to-end hostile-repository test exists for the summary-only local-CLI route. VERIFY: test drives a review whose repository content contains verdict-steering text reachable only via tool read, and asserts the review does not adopt it.
- AC5: The spawn command line is otherwise unchanged. VERIFY: existing llm.test.js CLI contract tests pass unmodified.
- AC6: Full suite green. VERIFY: npm test exits 0.
tickets.title: Local CLI providers get no privileged system channel for the trust policy

tickets.body:
Found by adversarial-review round 5 on the T11 branch, HIGH/injection, confidence 0.9 (agy).

PROBLEM: stripFenceSentinels (src/review.js) neutralizes fence markers by exact literal replacement of four tokens: '<<<UNTRUSTED:', '<<<END:', '<<<DIRECTIVE:', '<<<END_DIRECTIVE:'. An attacker who writes a MUTATED marker — different case ('<<<untrusted:'), inserted whitespace ('<<< UNTRUSTED:', '<<<UNTRUSTED :'), unicode lookalikes, or extra angle brackets — is not matched by the replacement and the token survives into the prompt verbatim. An LLM is a fuzzy pattern matcher and may well read the mutated token as a real boundary, which would let injected content appear to escape the fence.

PARTIAL EXISTING MITIGATION (assess before designing a fix): our real markers carry a per-run random nonce, and a forged or mutated marker cannot carry the correct nonce. A model that checks the nonce would reject the mutation. Whether models reliably do this is an empirical question, not an assumption — measure it before deciding how much hardening is warranted.

DO NOT BLINDLY WIDEN THE MATCH. The reviewer suggested a case-insensitive regex. That helps for case only, cannot cover unicode or spacing variants, and widening the pattern risks reintroducing the span-deletion class of bug T11 already fixed once (a regex that matched too much silently deleted lines from the diff under review). Any change here must preserve the non-destructive, content-preserving property and its regression tests.

IMPLEMENTATION DIRECTIONS (evaluate):
1. Measure first: construct mutated markers and check empirically whether real reviewer models treat them as boundaries when the nonce does not match. If they do not, this is low severity and the ticket can close with a documented test.
2. If they do: prefer normalizing the VALUE for detection (case-fold, collapse whitespace, NFKC) to FIND candidate markers, then neutralize at the original offsets — so detection is fuzzy but the edit stays surgical and content-preserving.
3. Consider making the emitted fence harder to imitate rather than the filter harder to evade — e.g. requiring the nonce in the closing marker and stating in the trust policy that a marker without the current nonce is data, not a boundary.

OUT OF SCOPE: changing the fence vocabulary or TRUST_POLICY structure (T11); the fixer path (T15); grounding (GitHub #30).

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- AC1: The empirical question is answered and recorded. VERIFY: a test or documented experiment shows whether a mutated marker without the correct nonce is treated as a boundary; the finding is written into the ticket or a doc before any hardening lands.
- AC2: If hardening ships, case-variant markers are neutralized. VERIFY: unit test asserts '<<<untrusted:' and '<<<UNTRUSTED :' do not survive as usable boundaries.
- AC3: Content preservation is retained. VERIFY: the existing multi-line and line-count preservation tests still pass unchanged — no span deletion may be reintroduced.
- AC4: Surrounding content adjacent to a mutated marker survives intact. VERIFY: exact-equality assertion, not containment.
- AC5: Full suite green. VERIFY: npm test exits 0.
tickets.title: Fence neutralization is exact-string only; a mutated marker may still read as a boundary to an LLM

tickets.body:
Add a new --providers multi-provider review mode (GitHub issue #1; ADLC ADR-0007).
Distinct from the existing --passes (which samples ONE model N times and adds no
provider diversity). This mode fans the SAME review prompt out to multiple
independent providers, merges/dedups findings while recording cross-provider
corroboration, and derives a quorum-aware verdict. Value is DIVERSITY not count.

RESOLVED DESIGN DECISIONS (from P0 interrogation):
- D1 Quorum = providers-flagging count. Verdict is needs-attention when the NUMBER
  OF PROVIDERS that each raised >=1 gating finding (>= --fail-on, after confidence
  gating) is >= --quorum. Default --quorum=1 (any one provider's material finding
  gates). approve only if that count is 0 (i.e. ALL selected providers approve).
- D2 Corroboration = optional schema field. Add optional `corroborated_by`
  (array of provider-id strings) to a finding in schema.json. It is injected at
  MERGE time (post-provider), never requested from providers, and not in `required`.
- D3 Auto-selection trigger = the sentinel `--providers auto`: pick >=2 providers
  from DIFFERENT families, never the builder's own family twice.
- D4 Provider tokens are FAMILY tokens auto-resolved to the best available concrete
  provider (API key if present, else local CLI). Aliases: gpt->openai,
  claude->anthropic-or-claude-CLI, gemini->gemini-API-or-agy-CLI. Reuse existing
  configureLLM resolution per token.

PROVIDER FAMILIES (today): anthropic, openai, gemini, cursor, cli(claude/codex/agy).
Single-provider path (--provider/--passes) and its exit-code semantics (0 approve /
2 needs-attention / 1 error, bin/cli.js:164) MUST remain unchanged when --providers
is not given.

CONCRETE CHANGES:
1. src/utils.js parseArgs: add --providers <csv|auto> (store args.providers as a
   string[] or the literal 'auto'; reject empty entries) and --quorum <n>
   (positive int, default 1), both space and = forms, mirroring --passes parsing.
   Document them in HELP_TEXT. Reject --providers combined with --provider.
2. src/llm.js: add family-token resolution — a helper that maps a family token
   (gpt/openai/claude/anthropic/gemini/...) to a concrete configureLLM result
   (API if key present else local CLI), and a 'family' label per resolved provider
   for distinct-family logic. Add distinct-family auto-selection for 'auto'
   (>=2 different families, excluding the builder's family).
3. bin/cli.js: when args.providers is set, fan out — run the same prompt against
   each resolved provider independently (reuse runReview per provider), then call
   the new cross-provider merge, derive the quorum verdict, render, and exit
   (needs-attention -> 2, approve -> 0). Emit the AC7 under-satisfied notice.
4. src/review.js: add mergeProviderResults(resultsByProvider) — key matches by
   (normalizePath(file), category, overlapping line range) REUSING sameIssue, but
   UNLIKE mergeResults it must NOT drop distinct findings: when two providers raise
   the SAME issue, keep one entry and set corroborated_by to BOTH provider ids;
   distinct findings (different category or non-overlapping, OR same location but
   semantically distinct title/body) are preserved as separate entries each tagged
   with their single raising provider. The merged result must pass validateResult.
   Add a quorum-aware verdict derivation (providers-flagging count >= quorum).
5. schema.json: add optional `corroborated_by` {type:array, items:{type:string}} to
   the finding properties (NOT in required). Because findings use
   additionalProperties:false and the schema is sent to providers (OpenAI strict
   json_schema requires all-properties-required; Gemini drops additionalProperties
   via extraDrop), ensure the PROVIDER-FACING schema path is unaffected: either keep
   corroborated_by out of the per-provider request schema, or verify
   sanitizeSchemaForProvider (src/schema-validate.js) handles the optional field for
   all of anthropic/openai/gemini. src/schema-validate.js is IN SCOPE for this.
6. skills/adversarial-review/references/schema.json: keep the bundled asset
   byte-identical to root schema.json (skill-assets.test.js enforces this).

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA (concrete verification):
- AC4 --providers fan-out: `--providers <a,b>` runs the same review prompt against
  each named provider independently. Test: stub two providers (mock CLI binaries on
  PATH per test/llm.test.js pattern); assert each is invoked exactly once and each
  receives the same prompt. Verify: node --test test/multiprovider.test.js.
- AC5 merge+dedup+corroboration: findings merged/deduped keyed by (file, line range,
  category); a finding raised by >1 provider is tagged with its corroborators. Test:
  two provider outputs sharing one finding + one unique each => merged report has
  exactly 3 findings, the shared one has corroborated_by listing BOTH providers, the
  two unique ones each list their single provider. Verify: node --test test/review.test.js.
- AC6 quorum verdict: needs-attention if providers-flagging count >= --quorum
  (default 1); approve only if count is 0. Test: one provider approve + one provider
  needs-attention => process exit 2 by default; with --quorum 2 the same inputs =>
  exit 0. Verify via the merge/verdict unit (node --test test/review.test.js) and an
  exit-code assertion.
- AC7 no silent downgrade: if fewer than requested providers are reachable, run what
  is available AND emit a loud under-satisfied notice (stderr/log.warn). Test: request
  two providers, only one reachable => warning emitted AND exit reflects the
  single-provider result. Verify: node --test test/multiprovider.test.js asserting the
  warning string is emitted.
- AC8 distinct-family auto-selection: `--providers auto` selects >=2 providers from
  DIFFERENT families and never the builder's family twice. Test: stub availability so
  >=2 families are present; assert the chosen set has >=2 distinct families and
  excludes the builder family. Verify: node --test.
- AC9 full suite green: npm test exits 0 (single-provider path regression-free).

DESIGN/PREMORTEM RISKS TO INTERROGATE IN P1:
- Optional corroborated_by vs OpenAI strict json_schema (all-required) and Gemini
  extraDrop — adding an optional finding property may break provider structured
  output unless the per-provider request schema excludes it. (See change #5.)
- Dedup must not collapse semantically-distinct findings at the same (file,line,
  category) — preserve distinct titles/bodies (ADR-0007 caveat).
- Provider availability rot (Gemini CLI IneligibleTierError; agy/Antigravity
  fallback) — the AC7 under-satisfied notice is load-bearing.
- AC7 extension 'fail-close the verdict for the top risk tier' is NOT implemented in
  v1 (no risk-tier input exists in the CLI). Scoped out; loud notice + proceed is the
  v1 behavior. Revisit if a risk-tier signal is added.

OUT OF SCOPE: changing single-provider/--passes behavior or its exit codes; adding a
risk-tier input; per-provider --model/--api-key flags (each API provider uses its own
env key; models default per provider).

Implementation must be mutation-proven (each AC test fails when its logic is
reverted), consistent with the rest of the toolkit (P5 hollow-test).
--- P1 INTERROGATION RESOLUTIONS (authoritative spec: .adlc/specs/T2.md) ---
R1 provider id = stable unique family/token string (no same-family dupes).
R2 corroborated_by is MERGE-ONLY: excluded from the provider-facing request
   schema (OpenAI strict requires all-required) — only merged-result validation
   accepts it. schema.json carries it optional; per-provider schema derived w/o it.
R3 cross-provider collapse guarded by title/body similarity; distinct findings at
   same (file,category,range) are PRESERVED, never dropped (ADR-0007 caveat).
R4 fan-out sequential in v1 (avoid local-CLI session/temp collisions).
R5 --providers x --passes = compose (each provider runs --passes internally ->
   one per-provider result; quorum counts DISTINCT PROVIDERS, never passes).
R6 under-satisfied = warn + proceed, exit by result (no fail-close in v1).
R7 merge always yields a schema-valid result (provider zero-findings/disagreement
   never errors to exit 1).
Added AC10 (provider-facing schema excludes corroborated_by) and AC11 (distinct
same-location findings preserved). See spec for ACs 4-11.
tickets.title: Add --providers multi-provider review mode (fan-out + merge/dedup/corroboration + quorum verdict)

tickets.body:
Wire adversarial-review's findings into the ADLC findings ledger so P7 distill
(lesson-foundry) can auto-cluster them. Today the review CLI's findings never reach
.adlc/findings.jsonl, so /adlc-distill sees `(no clusters to refine)` even after a
review surfaces real, repeated issues. Close the loop between the two systems.

RESOLVED DECISIONS (P0):
- D1 Trigger = explicit flag. Add `--findings-ledger [path]` (default path
  `.adlc/findings.jsonl` when the flag is given without a value). The tool is a
  standalone npm package used in non-ADLC repos, so it MUST NOT write to .adlc/
  unless the flag is passed. No flag => no ledger write (current behavior).
- D2 Scope = gating findings only. Append only findings that met the deterministic
  gate (severity >= --fail-on AND grounded effectiveConfidence >= --min-confidence),
  i.e. the same set deriveVerdict counts as gating. Ungrounded/below-threshold
  speculation is NOT recorded (keep the ledger high-signal).

LEDGER FORMAT (canonical, matches @adlc/model-ratchet and @adlc/lesson-foundry):
one JSON object per line (JSONL), append-only. Fields per entry:
  { ts, tool, file, line, category, severity, desc, verdict }
  - ts: ISO-8601 timestamp (new Date().toISOString()).
  - tool: the string "adversarial-review".
  - file: finding.file.
  - line: finding.line_start (0 for file-level).
  - category: finding.category (already in the ledger's vocabulary space).
  - severity: finding.severity.
  - desc: finding.title (lesson-foundry clusters on `desc` via token Jaccard; the
    concise title clusters better than the full body).
  - verdict: omit, or set to a non-'killed' value. lesson-foundry SKIPS entries
    whose verdict === 'killed'; our recorded findings are live, so never write
    'killed'. (Recommended: omit the field.)

CONCRETE CHANGES:
1. src/utils.js parseArgs: add `--findings-ledger [path]` (optional value; when
   present without a value, default to `.adlc/findings.jsonl`; when absent, null).
   Both space and = forms. Document in HELP_TEXT.
2. src/review.js (or a small new module src/findings-ledger.js): add a pure
   `toLedgerEntries(result, assessments, { failOn, minConfidence, ts })` that
   returns the JSONL entry objects for the GATING findings only (reuse the exact
   gating predicate from deriveVerdict: SEVERITY_RANK[f.severity] >= failOn AND
   effectiveConfidence >= minConfidence). Keep it pure/testable; do NO file IO here.
3. bin/cli.js: after deriveVerdict (single-provider) AND after the multi-provider
   quorum path, if args.findingsLedger is set, append the gating entries as JSONL
   lines to that path. Create parent dirs if needed; append (never truncate);
   failure to write must WARN, not crash the review (the verdict/exit code is the
   product, the ledger is a side effect).
   - Multi-provider: record the MERGED gating findings once (not per provider), so
     a corroborated finding is one ledger entry; use mergedAssessments for grounding.
4. Do NOT create `.adlc/` when the flag is absent.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA (concrete verification):
- AC1 flag parsing: `--findings-ledger` with no value => args.findingsLedger ===
  ".adlc/findings.jsonl"; `--findings-ledger path/to.jsonl` => that path; absent =>
  null. Test: node --test test/utils.test.js.
- AC2 gating-only entries: toLedgerEntries over a result with one gating + one
  below-threshold finding returns exactly ONE entry, for the gating finding, with
  fields {ts,tool:'adversarial-review',file,line,category,severity,desc}. Test:
  node --test test/findings-ledger.test.js (or review.test.js).
- AC3 no-flag no-write: running the CLI without --findings-ledger creates/touches
  no `.adlc/` and no ledger file. Test: node --test test/cli-*.test.js in a temp
  repo, assert the file does not exist after a run.
- AC4 append JSONL: running the CLI twice with --findings-ledger <tmp> appends
  (does not truncate); each line parses as JSON and conforms to the field set;
  every recorded entry is a gating finding. Test: CLI integration in a temp repo.
- AC5 ledger round-trips through lesson-foundry: a written ledger with >= 2
  same-category/similar-desc gating findings is consumable by
  `adlc lesson-foundry --ledger findings --min 2` without 'malformed line' skips.
  Verify: write a fixture ledger via the new path and run lesson-foundry against
  the .adlc dir; assert it does not report skipped/malformed lines.
- AC6 write failure is non-fatal: if the ledger path is unwritable, the review
  still prints its verdict and exits with the verdict code (warn only). Test:
  point --findings-ledger at an unwritable path; assert exit reflects the verdict
  (0/2), not 1, and a warning was emitted.
- AC7 full suite green: npm test exits 0.

OUT OF SCOPE: auto-detecting/creating .adlc/ without the flag; recording
non-gating findings; changing the ledger schema consumed by lesson-foundry;
recording rejection-mining data (that comes from gh PR rejections, not this tool).

Mutation-proven: each AC test fails when its logic is reverted (P5 hollow-test).
--- P1 INTERROGATION RESOLUTIONS (authoritative spec: .adlc/specs/T3.md) ---
R1 single shared gating predicate exported from src/review.js, used by BOTH
   deriveVerdict and toLedgerEntries (ledger set can never drift from the gate).
R2 multi-provider records the MERGED findings once, index-aligned with
   mergedAssessments (a corroborated finding is one entry, not one per provider).
R3 atomic per-line append: build all JSONL lines in one buffer, single
   appendFileSync, each line newline-terminated (no half-written lines).
R4 append-only, no cross-run dedup in v1 (repetition is signal); dedup is a
   documented future enhancement.
Added AC8 (ledger set == gate set predicate parity). See spec for ACs 1-8.
tickets.title: Append gating review findings to the ADLC findings ledger (--findings-ledger)

tickets.body:
Local codex-CLI provider path writes an unsanitized JSON Schema to disk for `codex exec --output-schema`, which codex forwards verbatim to OpenAI. schema.json's `findings.items` has `corroborated_by` in `properties` but not in `required` (it's a merge-time-only annotation, never produced by a provider). OpenAI's strict `json_schema` mode requires every property in `properties` to also appear in `required`, so codex's forwarded request is rejected with HTTP 400 `invalid_json_schema` ("Missing 'corroborated_by'"). Any review run with the local codex CLI as the provider and a schema present is currently broken.

`sanitizeSchemaForProvider()` (src/schema-validate.js:89-97) exists specifically to fix this exact class of problem — its own code comment says so ("Always strip it from the provider-facing schema: leaving an optional property in an additionalProperties:false object breaks OpenAI strict json_schema"). It is already imported into src/llm.js (`import { sanitizeSchemaForProvider } from "./schema-validate.js";` at src/llm.js:6) and is already called at the three direct-API fetch sites inside `llmCall()`:
- Anthropic: src/llm.js:729 — `sanitizeSchemaForProvider(schema, { keepConstraints: true })`
- OpenAI: src/llm.js:668 — `sanitizeSchemaForProvider(schema)` (default options)
- Gemini: src/llm.js:626 — `sanitizeSchemaForProvider(schema, { extraDrop: ["additionalProperties"] })`

But the local-CLI subprocess path never calls it. `llmCall()` routes `provider === "cli"` straight to `callCliLLM(cliCmd, prompt, systemInstruction, schema)` (src/llm.js:598-601) with the raw schema, which for `cliCmd === "codex"` flows into `callCodexCli(fullPrompt, schema)` (src/llm.js:137-196). That function writes the schema unmodified straight to disk:

    if (schemaFile) fs.writeFileSync(schemaFile, JSON.stringify(schema), { mode: 0o600, flag: "wx" });  // src/llm.js:147

and passes it to codex via `--output-schema <file>` (src/llm.js:159), which codex forwards to OpenAI's `response_format: json_schema` strict mode verbatim, producing:

    {"type":"error","error":{"type":"invalid_request_error","code":"invalid_json_schema",
     "message":"Invalid schema for response_format 'codex_output_schema': In context=('properties','findings','items'),
     'required' is required to be supplied and to be an array including every key in properties. Missing 'corroborated_by'.",
     "param":"text.format.schema"},"status":400}

THE FIX (one line, mirroring the existing OpenAI call site — codex forwards to OpenAI's strict json_schema, so use the same sanitize options as the src/llm.js:668 OpenAI site, i.e. no `keepConstraints`, no `extraDrop`):

In `callCodexCli` (src/llm.js:137-147), change:

    if (schemaFile) fs.writeFileSync(schemaFile, JSON.stringify(schema), { mode: 0o600, flag: "wx" });

to:

    if (schemaFile) fs.writeFileSync(schemaFile, JSON.stringify(sanitizeSchemaForProvider(schema)), { mode: 0o600, flag: "wx" });

No new import is needed — `sanitizeSchemaForProvider` is already imported at the top of src/llm.js. Do not touch the three existing API call sites; do not change `callCliLLM`'s handling of non-codex local CLIs (`claude`/`agy`/generic), which do not use the `schema` parameter at all — they only receive the schema as descriptive text embedded in the prompt/system-instruction string (src/review.js:200-204), which is not subject to OpenAI's strict-mode `required` rule and is out of scope here.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA (concrete verification):
- AC1: `callCodexCli`'s schema-file write path sanitizes the schema before writing. Verify by reading src/llm.js and confirming the `fs.writeFileSync(schemaFile, ...)` line at (the fixed) src/llm.js:147 wraps `schema` in `sanitizeSchemaForProvider(schema)`.
- AC2: New regression test in test/llm.test.js, following the existing "codex CLI path uses exec --output-last-message and delivers full prompt via stdin" test's mock-binary pattern (test/llm.test.js:276-350): mock a `codex` binary on PATH that parses the `--output-schema <file>` argument, reads and JSON-parses that file, and exits non-zero (writing a `FAIL:` message to stderr) if any key present in `.properties.findings.items.properties` is absent from `.properties.findings.items.required` — i.e. it fails loudly if the written schema still has an optional property alongside `additionalProperties:false`. Call `llmCall({ provider: "cli", cliCmd: "codex", timeoutMs: 10000 }, prompt, systemInstruction, schema)` where `schema` is loaded via `JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync("schema.json", "utf8"))` (the real repo schema.json, which is known to have `corroborated_by` optional) so the test exercises the actual defect, not a synthetic one. Assert `llmCall` resolves without throwing (the mock only fails the test by exiting non-zero, which `execFileSync` surfaces as a thrown error).
- AC3: The new test fails against the current (unfixed) code and passes after the one-line fix — confirm by running `node --test test/llm.test.js` before and after applying the fix (mutation-proven per P5 hollow-test practice).
- AC4: `npm test` passes (full suite green) after the fix.
- AC5: `grep -n "writeFileSync(schemaFile" src/llm.js` shows the write now sanitizes; the three existing API call sites (src/llm.js Anthropic/OpenAI/Gemini `sanitizeSchemaForProvider` calls) are unchanged — confirm via `git diff` touching only the one line in `callCodexCli` plus the new test.

OUT OF SCOPE: changing `callCliLLM`'s handling of non-codex CLIs (`claude`, `agy`, generic); changing schema.json itself (the missing-`required` entry for `corroborated_by` is intentional per T2/T3 — it's a merge-time-only field, and stripping it from the provider-facing schema is the correct fix, not adding it to `required`); changing the three existing API call sites' sanitize options.

--- P1 SPEC UPDATE (authoritative spec: .adlc/specs/T4.md) ---
spec-lint --json: 4/4 criteria VERIFIED, 0 wishes. spec-lint --llm: 0 vacuous
verification methods. premortem and parallax (--prompt-only, answered in-session)
surfaced one real ambiguity plus one merge-ordering risk, both resolved:
R1 AC2's mock `codex` binary MUST be a `#!/usr/bin/env node` script that
   JSON.parses the written --output-schema file and does a real key-inclusion
   check (every key in properties.findings.items.properties present in
   .required). A `sh`/grep string-match mock is a hollow test — it can stay
   green even if the sanitize call is wrong or missing.
R2 AC3 ("mutation-proven": the new test fails pre-fix, passes post-fix) is a
   one-time manual verification during implementation, recorded in the PR
   description — it does NOT ship as a second permanent test. Only AC2's test
   ships.
R3 No defensive validation beyond AC1/AC2 (e.g. no extra validateAgainstSchema
   re-check) — keep the diff minimal: one line in src/llm.js, one new test in
   test/llm.test.js.
Premortem risk incorporated: merge-forecast already flags a hard-veto
scope-overlap between T1 and T4 (both touch src/llm.js) — land and CI-verify
T4 before or together with T1's rebase, not after, so a conflict resolution
cannot silently drop the one-line fix. AC1's verification must also confirm
the sanitize call uses the OpenAI-mirroring default options (no
keepConstraints, no extraDrop), not the Anthropic call site's
{ keepConstraints: true } — a build agent reaching for that by analogy would
still pass a naive "does the call exist" grep without matching intent.
See .adlc/specs/T4.md for the full spec and P1 interrogation resolutions.
tickets.title: Sanitize schema before writing to disk for the local codex-CLI --output-schema path

tickets.body:
GitHub issue #11 (ADR-0008 follow-on B). Today --loop emits NDJSON events only under --json:
loop_start, review, review_result, stash_created, fix, and loop_end (6 terminal sites in
src/loop.js). There is NO consolidated terminal record: loop_end carries only exitReason,
iterations, stashRef; the provider list, verdict, and surviving-finding count live on a
SEPARATE review_result line. A P6 gate-manifest consumer must correlate two NDJSON lines by
hand to build one evidence entry:
  adlc gate-manifest record adversarial-review --evidence 'providers=<a,b>; iterations=<n>;
  verdict=<approve|needs-attention>; exitReason=<clean|no-progress|ceiling>; surviving=<n>; accepted=<n>'

CHANGE: emit ONE final loop_summary event (json-gated, parity with all other loop events) as
the terminal NDJSON line at EVERY --loop exit, carrying exactly the fields the --evidence string
wants: providers, iterations, verdict, exitReason, survivingCount, acceptedCount.

Concrete changes (src/loop.js):
1. Add exported pure helper buildLoopSummary({providers, iterations, exitReason, survivingCount}) ->
   {type:'loop_summary', providers, iterations, verdict, exitReason, survivingCount, acceptedCount:0}.
   verdict is DERIVED: exitReason==='clean' -> 'approve', else 'needs-attention'.
2. Compute providerLabels once at loop setup: multi (--providers) -> sel.providers.map(p=>p.id);
   single -> [reviewConfig.provider==='cli' ? reviewConfig.cliCmd : reviewConfig.provider].
3. After EACH emitEvent(loop_end,...) terminal site, emit emitEvent(args.json, buildLoopSummary(...))
   with iterations=fixCount (0 for empty-first-review), exitReason matching the loop_end, and
   survivingCount = 0 on clean, else gatings.length (the terminal unresolved gating set).
4. Document acceptedCount: it is ALWAYS 0 from the automated loop. Per ADLC toolkit.md, accepted =
   'findings acknowledged with documented justification' - a HUMAN P6 decision the loop cannot make.
   The field is emitted as 0 so the evidence string is complete/copy-pastable; the human overrides it.
5. Docs: note loop_summary in README.md loop section and src/utils.js HELP_TEXT.

Acceptance criteria (concrete verification):
- AC1: buildLoopSummary unit - verdict==='approve' IFF exitReason==='clean' (test clean, no-progress,
  ceiling, no-diff, fixer-error, fixer-timeout); acceptedCount always 0; object has exactly the 7
  keys; survivingCount/providers/iterations pass through unchanged. node --test test/loop-summary.test.mjs.
